texto: In the early nineties, I was a cub reporter on a city newspaper in Limerick, and assigned to the courthouse there. One day, an old detective sergeant came and whispered to me in the press pit. He pointed out a young offender, a teenager who was up for stealing a car or something relatively minor, and said, 'See this kid? He'll kill.'
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Kevin Barry que provoca temas ligados a reflexao. Ao questionar “In the early nineties, I was a cub reporter on a city newspaper in Limerick, and assigned to the …”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, day, city, com foco em termino e city.

texto: Before forty, you think that exhaustion is something like a long-lasting hangover. But at forty you learn all about it. Even your passions exhaust you.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Kevin Barry: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a reflexao. O trecho “Before forty, you think that exhaustion is something like a long-lasting hangover. But at forty y…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, aging, exhaustion pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de exhaustion e forty.

texto: One might trouble one's dainty snout with a whiff of the taleggio displayed in an artisanal cheese shop, or take a saucer of jasmine tea and a knuckle of fennel-scented snuff at a counter of buffed Big Nothing granite. But there was a want in these ladies yet, and it was for the rude life of youth.
explicacao: Citação de Kevin Barry sobre sentido da existência e experiência vivida. “One might trouble one's dainty snout with a whiff of the taleggio displayed in an artisanal chees…” articula reflexao, exotic, exotic-sensory-experience em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como exotic-sensory-experience e flavor.

frase_original: One might trouble one's dainty snout with a whiff of the taleggio displayed in an artisanal cheese shop, or take a saucer of jasmine tea and a knuckle of fennel-scented snuff at a counter of buffed Big Nothing granite. But there was a want in these ladies yet, and it was for the rude life of youth.

texto: And the view was suddenly clear to me. The world opened out to its grim beyonds and I realized that, at forty, one must learn the rigors of acceptance. Capitalize it: Acceptance. I needed to accept what was put before me--be it a watery grave in Ireland's only natural fjord, or a return to the city and its grayer intensities, or a wordless exile in some steaming Cambodian swamp hole, or poems or no poems, or children or not, lovers or not, illness or otherwise, success or its absence. I would accept all that was put in my way, from here on through until I breathed my last.

The world opened out to its grim beyonds and I realized th…” funciona como lembrete prático ligado a sabedoria, acceptance, aging, útil em momentos de aging e clarity.
